data.title: Indonesia, Freeport units sign MoU to extend mining permit beyond 2041
data.description: Indonesia's investment minister and miners Freeport-McMoran and its Indonesia unit Freeport Indonesia have signed a memorandum of understanding to extend Freeport Indonesia's mining permit beyond 2041, Freeport-McMoran chairman Richard Adkerson said.

data.title: Nikkei Gains 0.5%, Led by Chip, Metals Stocks — Market Talk
data.description:
1912 ET - Japanese stocks are higher following overnight gains in tech stocks on Wall Street as fears about disruption caused by artificial intelligence ease. Chip and metals stocks are leading the gains in early trade. Kioxia Holdings is up 4.0% and Mitsui Kinzoku is 4.1% higher. USD/JPY is at 154.64, compared with 153.44 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are closely watching Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's economic measures as well as nuclear talks between Iran and the U.S. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 0.5% at 57436.50. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

1912 ET - JGB futures fall in early Tokyo trade, tracking overnight price declines in the U.S. Treasury market. JGBs and Treasurys tend to move in tandem. The focus is likely to be on the Japanese Finance Ministry's auction today of about 800 billion yen of 20-year JGBs. There appears to be room for price gains for super-long JGB sector, however, demand could be temporary and will probably ebb by end-March, Citi Research's Tomohisa Fujiki says in a research report. "We are likely to see another major correction in April-June when fiscal policy will be the focus," the rates strategist adds. March 10-year JGB futures are 0.10 yen lower at 132.38 yen. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1846 ET - Japanese stocks may get a lift from overnight tech stock gains on Wall Street and fears easing about the disruption caused by artificial intelligence. Nikkei futures are up 0.6% at 57580 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 154.76, compared with 153.44 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's policy steps to support the economy as well as nuclear talks between Iran and the U.S. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 1.0% to 57143.84 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1830 ET - Australian employment data for January at 0030 GMT may inject a little volatility into markets given its importance to the Reserve Bank of Australia and tendency to deliver the unexpected. Australian economists expect a rise in employment of about 15,000 for the month and for the unemployment rate to increase to 4.2% from 4.1% in December. The RBA has described the job market as too tight, and used that assumption to support an interest rate rise at the start of February. Another interest hike is expected by the market in May, with the swap market pricing in an 80% probability of a hike. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X@JamesGlynnWSJ)

1546 ET - Treasury yields rise amid signs of declining demand for U.S. debt while Fed minutes show no rush to cut interest rates. The Treasury sells nearly $16 billion in 20-year bonds. The participation of indirect bidders, which includes foreign central banks, falls to 55.2% from 64.7% in January. The bid-to-cover ratio slips to 2.36 from 2.86, indicating wobbling demand. Tomorrow, weekly jobless claims are expected to decline to 223,000 from 227,000, in a WSJ survey. December trade deficit is forecast to narrow to $55.5 billion from November's $56.8 billion. The 10-year yield rises 0.027 percentage point to 4.080%. The two-year adds 0.024 p.p. to 3.460%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1505 ET - The U.S., Canada, and Mexico are supposed to conduct a "joint review" of the USMCA on the sixth anniversary of the deal, which would be July 2026. They may renew or extend the deal, but with President Trump and Canadian Prime Minister Carney exchanging barbs in Davos, some analysts are nervous. Capital Economics say in a note that "the potential for backlash from an increasingly assertive Congress and the likelihood of intervention by the Supreme Court," is likely to prevent the U.S. from breaking the deal, but don't rule out that Trump may attempt a unilateral withdrawal. That would inject new uncertainty into markets that have already been holding on by a thread, such as commodities. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1443 ET - More than rough winter weather may be behind the retreat in Canada's housing market last month. Home sales fell 5.8% on-month in January, which National Bank's Daren King notes is a third consecutive decline and the sharpest drop since February 2025 when U.S. tariffs were announced. It left the level of transactions at the lowest since November 2023. And while there was bad weather in Ontario and Quebec, King says the data shows a widespread decline weather alone can't explain. For the first time since May 2021, activity was down in all Canadian provinces, the economist says. King says renewed uncertainty on the trade front probably explains the decline, and the market may regain momentum in the coming months if trade tensions ease. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1440 ET - Many economists believe that data-collection challenges caused by last fall's government shutdown artificially suppressed the year-over-year CPI inflation rate. The Federal Reserve's staff of economic researchers share that view, according to the minutes of the Fed's January meeting. The shutdown issues "likely pushed down the levels of the CPI and the PCE price index in November and December," the minutes say. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1425 ET - Minutes of January's FOMC meeting show participants generally expected that the effects of tariffs on core goods prices would likely start to diminish this year. "Several" participants remarked that the ongoing moderation in inflation for housing services was likely to continue to exert downward pressure on overall inflation. Meanwhile, several participants expected that higher productivity growth associated with tech or regulatory developments would put downward pressure on inflation. (jessica.coacci@wsj.com; @jessica_coacci)

1419 ET - In the minutes of the January FOMC meeting, "most" participants noted recent jobs data such as the unemployment rate, layoffs and job openings suggest labor market conditions may be stabilizing after a period of gradual cooling. Meanwhile, "several" participants noted their business contacts continued to express caution in hiring decisions — with uncertainty about the economic outlook and effect of AI. In addition, "some" participants pointed to labor supply such as lower net immigration as contributing to low pace of job gains. (jessica.coacci@wsj.com; @jessica_coacci)

1415 ET - At the Fed's January meeting, the manager of the Fed's financial-market operations, Roberto Perli, noted that the dollar depreciated in January after the New York Fed's trading desk made "rate checks" in the dollar-yen currency pair--a development that was reported by The Wall Street Journal and others at the time. Perli reiterated at the January Fed meeting that the rate checks were made at the direction of the U.S. Treasury, for which the New York Fed serves as fiscal agent. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1414 ET - When the Fed met in January, "several" officials in the meeting wanted the Fed's communications after the meeting to signal more neutral ambiguity about whether the central bank's next rate move would be another cut or a hike. That is a hawkish tidbit, because traders have broadly priced in the probability of further cuts ahead, with little thought to potential hikes. Not all officials were on board with that view--"several" said they believe more cuts will be appropriate if inflation continues to cool. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

data.title: CME cattle futures dip after following the cash trade up
data.description:
Chicago Mercantile Exchange cattle futures ticked down on Wednesday after climbing on Tuesday, taking their cue from the cash markets, according to analysts.

CME April live cattle futures (LCJ26) settled 0.275 cent lower to 242.525 cents per pound. March feeder cattle (FCH26) ended 0.400 cent lower at 370.575 cents per pound.

Beef packers lost $345.45 per head on Wednesday, compared with losses of $318.95 per head on Tuesday, according to livestock marketing advisory service HedgersEdge.com

The U.S. Department of Agriculture reported 113,000 cattle were slaughtered on Wednesday.

Choice cuts of beef were priced at $363.80 per hundredweight, down 96 cents from Tuesday, according to USDA data.

Select cuts of beef rose $0.41 to $360.63 per cwt. Consumer demand for beef has remained strong, despite record-high beef prices.

Lean hog futures firmed, having swung back and forth over the previous weeks on concerns about winter weather and diseases in the herd, according to Altin Kalo, head economist at Steiner Consulting Group.

In boxed pork, the USDA reported pork carcasses at $95.56 per cwt, down $0.32.

CME April hogs (LHJ26) settled 0.250 cent higher at 92.55 cents per pound.

data.title: Japan Core Machinery Orders Jump 19.1% In December
data.description:
TOKYO (dpa-AFX) - The value of core machinery orders in Japan was up a seasonally adjusted19.1 percent on month in December, the Cabinet Office said on Thursday - coming in at 1,052.5 billion yen.

That beat forecasts for an increase of 5,1 percent following the 11.0 percent contraction in November.

On a yearly basis, orders jumped 16.8 percent - again exceeding expectations for a gain of 3.9 percent after sinking 6.4 percent in the previous month.

For the fourth quarter of 2025, orders were up 7.9 percent on quarter and 8.1 percent on year.

For the first quarter of 2026, orders are seen lower by 4.5 percent on quarter but up 2.2 percent on year.

Total orders surged 23.8 percent on month and 40.1 percent on year to 4,148.1 billion yen.

data.title: South Korea braces for verdict in ex‑President Yoon's insurrection trial
data.description:
Key points:
  • Prosecutors seek death penalty for insurrection
  • Yoon's martial law attempt lasted only six hours, but triggered a political crisis
  • Seoul court to rule on the most serious charges Yoon has faced
  • No execution has been carried out in South Korea since 1997

By Joyce Lee

A South Korean court is due to rule on Thursday on whether former President Yoon Suk Yeol is guilty of masterminding an insurrection over his botched attempt to put the country under martial law in December 2024.

The verdict by the Seoul Central District Court is the most consequential yet for the ousted leader, whose attempt to impose emergency rule triggered a national political crisis and tested the resilience of the country’s democratic institutions.

Prosecutors sought the death penalty in January, saying "his unconstitutional and illegal emergency martial law undermined the function of the National Assembly and the Election Commission... actually destroying the liberal democratic constitutional order."

Masterminding an insurrection carries a maximum sentence of the death penalty or life imprisonment under South Korean law.

South Korea last handed down a death sentence in 2016, but has not executed anyone since 1997.

There was a strong police presence at the Seoul Central District Court, with police buses forming a security cordon around the building.

The court will also rule on charges that Yoon abused his authority by ordering troops to storm parliament to drag his opponents out and jail them, as well as dispatching soldiers and police to block, inspect, and control access to facilities such as the opposition party building.

Yoon, 65, has denied the charges. The conservative former career prosecutor argued he had presidential authority to declare martial law and that his action was aimed at sounding the alarm over opposition parties' obstruction of government.

SEOUL DETENTION CENTRE

The ousted former leader, who has been detained at the Seoul Detention Centre, is likely to remain there regardless of the ruling. If found guilty, he is expected to appeal the ruling, and if cleared his legal woes are far from over.

Yoon, who faces eight trial proceedings, received a five-year jail sentence in January in a separate trial on charges including obstructing attempts by authorities to arrest him following his martial law declaration. He has appealed the ruling.

While Yoon's bid to impose martial law lasted only about six hours before it was met by large street protests and voted down by parliament, it sent shockwaves through South Korea, which is Asia's fourth-largest economy, a key U.S. security ally, and long considered one of the world's most resilient democracies.

South Korea's current President Lee Jae Myung, who came to power in a snap election in June after Yoon was ousted, in a post on X on Thursday commended the actions of the Korean people to thwart the attempt to bring in martial law.

"It was possible because it was the Republic of Korea", Lee said, using South Korea's official title, adding the Korean people would serve as an example for human history.

His post was attached to a newspaper story about how some academics had recommended that the Korean public be put up for a Nobel Peace Prize for facing off against troops and police to oppose martial law without violence.

data.title: UK retailers' labour cost fears jump after reforms, survey shows
data.description:
Key points:
  • UK's Employment Rights Act became law in December
  • Secondary legislation has gone out to consultation
  • Retailers concerned about extra costs

British retailers have grown markedly more anxious about rising labour costs over the next year after new employment rights began to be implemented, a trade body survey of finance chiefs showed on Thursday.

Prime Minister Keir Starmer's Labour government secured parliamentary approval for its Employment Rights Act in December.

The government has called the legislation the biggest upgrade to UK workers' rights in a generation, arguing it is the best way to avoid the industrial action that has disrupted services in recent years.

While original plans for protections against unfair dismissal for new workers were softened, new rules on sick pay, zero-hours contracts and union rights remain. Secondary legislation for the Act has gone out for consultation.

FINANCE CHIEFS SAY LABOUR COSTS A TOP CONCERN

Trade body the British Retail Consortium (BRC) said its survey of finance chiefs found 84% ranked labour and employment costs in their top three concerns, up from 21% last July.

The BRC said 61% plan to reduce the number of hours staff work and/or reduce overtime and 45% would need to freeze recruitment.

It said 55% plan to reduce head office headcount and 42% plan to reduce stores headcount. It said 68% will try to make up for a smaller workforce by driving higher productivity, while 61% will invest in automation.

The retail industry is Britain's biggest private sector employer, but has shed 250,000 jobs over the last five years, according to the BRC.

The BRC said the risk does not lie with the Employment Rights Act's aims, but in how it is implemented.

"If the Government fails to consider business needs on policies including guaranteed hours and union rights, they will add complexity and reduce flexibility, ultimately stripping away entry-level and part-time opportunities at precisely the moment the country needs them most," BRC CEO Helen Dickinson said.

Several UK retailers, including John Lewis and Sainsbury's , have recently announced above-inflation pay rises for their workers that partly reflect another hike in the main government mandated minimum wage.

A survey published on Monday by the Chartered Institute of Personnel and Development found that more than one in three UK employers plan to cut their hiring of permanent staff due to costs introduced by the labour law reforms.

data.title: Key facts: XRP Payment Volume Drops 90%; Ripple Focuses on Tokenization
data.description:
• XRP's payment activity has plummeted, with on-chain data showing a nearly 90% drop in payment volume over the last two weeks after a brief surge to multibillion levels.1
  • Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse emphasized XRP as the company's main focus, referring to it as a 'North Star' at the XRP Community Day, which featured updates on tokenization and new leadership.2
  • Major firms like BlackRock and Mastercard are testing the XRP network for tokenized assets and settlement flows, evaluating on-chain liquidity and programmable money to enhance traditional systems.3

data.title: Key facts: MSTR's Saylor buys 2,486 Bitcoin; long-term holders surge; BTC stable at $67,600
data.description:
• Michael Saylor of MSTR announced the purchase of 2,486 Bitcoin for about $168.4 million, raising total holdings to 717,131 Bitcoin, with plans for quarterly acquisitions.1
  • Recent data shows a significant rise in Bitcoin (BTC) accumulation, with long-term holders increasing purchases to an average of 372,000 BTC monthly, up from 10,000 BTC in September 2024.2
  • Bitcoin (BTCUSD) is stable at $67,600, despite a 27% drop in the last month, as strong holder groups indicate aggressive selling, hinting at possible further declines.3

data.title: Key facts: Solana's Price Drops 72%; Tokenization Strategies Discussed; Zora Launches Markets
data.description:
• Solana's price fell to $67, down 72% from its peak of $295. It now trades between $80 and $90, with support at $78-$80, indicating potential further declines.12
  • On February 19, 2026, Solana discussed strategies with OnRe Finance, Loopscale, and Exponent Finance to tokenize yield-bearing assets, aiming to attract funds for on-chain fixed income.3
  • Zora has launched attention markets on Solana, causing backlash from the Base community after its previous support for Base. Despite this, Zora's tools still work on Base.4

data.title: Key facts: ETHZilla reduces Ethereum holdings; DEX trading volume falls
data.description:
• ETHZilla cut its Ethereum holdings from over 100,000 ETH to 69,802 ETH, selling about $40 million in October and $74.5 million in December to meet financial needs.12
  • Ethereum's daily trading volume on DEXs dropped to $1.5 billion from $3.72 billion, indicating reduced trading activity, while investment products experienced net outflows of $85.1 million.3
